questions.title: Java Compilation Command
questions.description: Which command is used to compile a Java source file named HelloWorld.java?
questions.explanation: The 'javac' command is the Java compiler. It takes a .java source file and compiles it into bytecode, producing a .class file. The 'java' command is used to run an already-compiled program, not to compile it.
hints: The compiler command name stands for 'Java Compiler'
Think about the distinction between compiling and running

questions.title: Running a Compiled Java Program
questions.description: After compiling HelloWorld.java, which command runs the program?
questions.explanation: The 'java' command launches the JVM and runs a compiled Java program. You provide the class name without the .class extension. Writing 'java HelloWorld.class' is a common mistake — you should not include the .class extension.
hints: You do not include the file extension when running a Java program
The 'java' command takes the class name, not the file name

questions.title: Java Source File Extension
questions.description: What is the file extension for a Java source code file?
questions.explanation: Java source code files use the .java extension. After compilation, the bytecode is stored in .class files. A .jar file is a Java Archive that bundles multiple .class files together for distribution.
hints: Source files contain human-readable code before compilation
The extension matches the language name

questions.title: Output of Java Bytecode Compilation
questions.description: When you compile a Java source file, what type of file is produced?
questions.explanation: The Java compiler (javac) produces .class files that contain Java bytecode. This bytecode is platform-independent and is executed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Unlike C/C++, Java does not compile directly to machine-specific executable files.
hints: Java uses an intermediate representation that runs on a virtual machine
The output is not platform-specific machine code

questions.title: What is the JVM?
questions.description: What does JVM stand for, and what is its primary role?
options: Java Virtual Machine — it executes Java bytecode
Java Variable Manager — it manages memory allocation
Java Version Monitor — it tracks Java versions
Java Visual Maker — it creates graphical interfaces
questions.explanation: JVM stands for Java Virtual Machine. It is the runtime engine that executes Java bytecode (.class files). The JVM is what makes Java platform-independent: the same bytecode can run on any system that has a compatible JVM installed, following the 'write once, run anywhere' principle.
hints: It acts as an abstraction layer between Java programs and the operating system
It is the reason Java programs can run on multiple platforms

questions.title: JDK vs JRE
questions.description: What is the difference between JDK and JRE?
options: JDK is for running programs; JRE is for developing programs
JDK includes development tools (like javac) plus the JRE; JRE only includes the JVM and libraries to run programs
JDK is an older version of Java; JRE is the newer version
JDK and JRE are two different names for the same thing
questions.explanation: The JDK (Java Development Kit) is a superset of the JRE (Java Runtime Environment). The JDK includes everything in the JRE plus development tools such as the Java compiler (javac), debugger (jdb), and documentation generator (javadoc). If you only need to run Java programs, the JRE is sufficient, but to develop Java programs you need the JDK.
hints: One is for development, the other is just for running
Think about what tools a developer needs that an end-user does not

questions.title: Correct main Method Signature
questions.description: Which is the correct signature for the main method in Java?
questions.explanation: The correct main method signature is 'public static void main(String[] args)'. It must be public (accessible from outside), static (callable without creating an object), void (returns nothing), and accept a String array parameter. If any of these parts are changed, the JVM will not recognize it as the entry point.
hints: The main method must be accessible by the JVM without instantiating the class
It does not return a value to the operating system like in C

questions.title: Printing to Console
questions.description: Which statement correctly prints "Hello, World!" followed by a newline in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, System.out.println() is the standard way to print text to the console followed by a newline. 'System' is a class in java.lang, 'out' is a static PrintStream field, and 'println' is a method that prints a line. Unlike Python's print() or C#'s Console.writeLine(), Java uses this fully qualified approach.
hints: Java uses the System class for standard I/O
'out' refers to the standard output stream

questions.title: println vs print
questions.description: What is the difference between System.out.println() and System.out.print()?
options: print() adds a newline after the output; println() does not
println() adds a newline after the output; print() does not
println() can only print strings; print() can print any type
There is no difference between them
questions.explanation: System.out.println() prints the argument and then moves the cursor to the next line (adds a newline character). System.out.print() prints the argument but keeps the cursor on the same line. Both methods can print any data type. The 'ln' in println stands for 'line'.
hints: The 'ln' suffix in println gives a clue about what it does
Think about what happens to the cursor position after each call

questions.title: Java Class and File Naming
questions.description: If a Java file contains a public class named MyProgram, what must the file be named?
options: The file can be named anything
questions.explanation: In Java, the file name must exactly match the name of the public class it contains, including capitalization, and must have the .java extension. So a public class named MyProgram must be in a file called MyProgram.java. This is a strict rule enforced by the Java compiler.
hints: Java is case-sensitive, including file names
There is a strict naming convention between public classes and files

questions.title: Java Statement Terminator
questions.description: How must every statement in Java be terminated?
questions.explanation: Every statement in Java must end with a semicolon (;). This is a syntactic requirement of the language. Forgetting a semicolon is one of the most common compilation errors for beginners. Unlike Python, Java does not use newlines to delimit statements.
hints: This is similar to C, C++, and C#
Forgetting this character is one of the most common beginner errors

questions.title: Scanner Import Statement
questions.description: Which import statement is needed to use the Scanner class for reading console input?
options: No import is needed — Scanner is in java.lang
questions.explanation: The Scanner class is located in the java.util package, so you must write 'import java.util.Scanner;' at the top of your file. Unlike classes in java.lang (such as String and System), classes in java.util are not automatically imported and must be explicitly imported.
hints: Scanner is a utility class for parsing input
Only java.lang classes are automatically available without import

questions.title: Creating a Scanner Object
questions.description: Which line correctly creates a Scanner object to read from the keyboard?
questions.explanation: To read from the keyboard, you create a Scanner object with System.in as the argument. System.in represents the standard input stream (keyboard). System.out is the output stream, not the input stream. Scanner requires an input source in its constructor, so 'new Scanner()' without arguments would not compile.
hints: System.in represents the standard input (keyboard)
Scanner needs to know which input source to read from

questions.title: Reading an Integer with Scanner
questions.description: Given 'Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);', which method reads an integer from the user?
questions.explanation: The Scanner method for reading an integer is nextInt(). Scanner uses the naming pattern next___() for its reading methods: nextInt(), nextDouble(), nextLine(), next(), etc. There is no readInt(), getInt(), or parseInt() method in the Scanner class.
hints: Scanner methods follow a consistent naming convention with 'next'
Think about the method names for other types like nextDouble()

questions.title: Java's Write Once Run Anywhere
questions.description: What does Java's 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' (WORA) principle mean?
options: Java programs are automatically distributed to all platforms
Java code does not need to be compiled
Java programs can only be written once and cannot be modified
Java bytecode can run on any platform that has a JVM installed
questions.explanation: The WORA principle means that once Java code is compiled to bytecode, it can run on any platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, etc.) that has a compatible JVM. The JVM abstracts away the differences between operating systems, making the bytecode portable. The source code still needs to be compiled, but only once.
hints: This is related to how bytecode and the JVM interact
Think about why Java uses an intermediate representation

questions.title: Java Comments — Single Line
questions.description: Which of the following is a valid single-line comment in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, single-line comments start with //. Everything after // on that line is ignored by the compiler. The # symbol is used for comments in Python and shell scripts. The /* symbol starts a multi-line comment but requires a closing */. The -- symbol is used in SQL.
hints: Java shares this comment syntax with C, C++, and C#
There are two types of comments in Java: single-line and multi-line

questions.title: Java is Object-Oriented
questions.description: Which of the following best describes Java as a programming language?
options: A procedural language like C with no support for objects
A purely functional programming language with no state
An object-oriented programming language where (almost) everything is organized into classes and objects
questions.explanation: Java is fundamentally an object-oriented programming language. In Java, all code must be written inside classes, and programs are built by creating objects that interact with each other. While Java supports some procedural and functional features, its core paradigm is object-oriented. Primitive types (int, double, etc.) are the exception — they are not objects.
hints: Think about what must contain every piece of Java code
Consider the role of classes in Java programs

questions.title: Primitive Type — int
questions.description: What is the size of the 'int' primitive type in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, an int is always 32 bits (4 bytes), regardless of the platform. This is different from C/C++, where the size of int can vary between platforms. Java guarantees fixed sizes for all primitive types, which is part of its platform independence. An int can hold values from -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.
hints: Java guarantees the same size for primitive types on all platforms
This is different from C where sizes can vary

questions.title: Boolean Type in Java
questions.description: Which of the following is a valid boolean literal in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, the boolean literals are 'true' and 'false' (all lowercase). Unlike C/C++, Java does not treat 1 and 0 as boolean values. 'True' with a capital T is not a valid boolean literal (unlike Python). Java's boolean type is strict — it only accepts true or false.
hints: Java is case-sensitive, even for literals
Java booleans are not interchangeable with integers

questions.title: String Declaration in Java
questions.description: Which of the following correctly declares and initializes a String variable in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, String is a class (capital S), and string literals are enclosed in double quotes. 'string' (lowercase) is not a type in Java. Single quotes are for char literals only, not strings. A char array and a String are different types in Java and cannot be assigned interchangeably.
hints: String is a class in Java, and class names are capitalized
Single quotes and double quotes have different meanings in Java

questions.title: Compilation Error vs Runtime Error
questions.description: Which of the following errors would be caught at compile time (by javac) rather than at runtime?
options: Missing semicolon at the end of a statement
questions.explanation: A missing semicolon is a syntax error that the Java compiler (javac) detects during compilation. The program cannot be compiled until this is fixed. Division by zero, array index out of bounds, and null pointer access are all runtime errors — they occur while the program is running and result in exceptions.
hints: The compiler checks syntax and type rules before the program can run
Errors that depend on actual data values cannot be detected at compile time

questions.title: What is Bytecode?
questions.description: What is Java bytecode?
options: Machine code specific to the operating system
The original Java source code stored in a .java file
An intermediate representation of Java code that is executed by the JVM
A compressed version of the Java source code
questions.explanation: Java bytecode is the intermediate instruction set that the Java compiler produces from source code. It is stored in .class files and is not specific to any hardware platform. The JVM interprets or JIT-compiles this bytecode into native machine code at runtime. This two-step process (source -> bytecode -> machine code) is what enables Java's platform independence.
hints: Bytecode sits between source code and native machine code
It is designed for a virtual machine, not real hardware

questions.title: Java main Method — Why Static?
questions.description: Why must the main method in Java be declared as 'static'?
options: So the method cannot be overridden by subclasses
So the JVM can call it without creating an instance of the class
So the method runs faster than non-static methods
So the method can access instance variables directly
questions.explanation: The main method must be static because it is the entry point of the program, and the JVM needs to call it before any objects have been created. A non-static method requires an instance of the class to be invoked, but at program startup, no objects exist yet. By making main static, the JVM can invoke it directly on the class itself.
hints: Think about what exists before the program starts running
Static methods belong to the class, not to an instance

questions.title: What Happens When main is Missing
questions.description: What happens if you compile and try to run a Java class that does not contain a main method?
options: The code compiles successfully, but a runtime error occurs when you try to run it
A compilation error occurs because every class must have a main method
The program runs but produces no output
The JVM automatically generates a default main method
questions.explanation: A Java class without a main method will compile without errors — not every class needs a main method. However, if you try to run that class with the 'java' command, the JVM will throw an error: 'Main method not found in class'. Only the class you try to run as the entry point needs a main method.
hints: Compilation and execution are separate steps in Java
The compiler does not require main — it just checks syntax and types

questions.title: Scanner — nextLine() After nextInt()
questions.description: What is a common problem when calling nextLine() immediately after nextInt() on a Scanner?
options: nextLine() reads the leftover newline character and returns an empty string
nextInt() consumes the newline, so there is no issue
nextLine() blocks and waits for new input indefinitely
questions.explanation: When you call nextInt(), it reads the integer but leaves the newline character (from pressing Enter) in the input buffer. The subsequent nextLine() call reads this leftover newline and returns an empty string, appearing to skip the input. The solution is to add an extra sc.nextLine() call after nextInt() to consume the leftover newline before reading the actual line of text.
hints: nextInt() does not consume the newline character after the number
Think about what remains in the input buffer after reading an integer

questions.title: What is an IDE?
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of an IDE (Integrated Development Environment) like IntelliJ IDEA or Eclipse for Java development?
options: To convert Java code directly to machine code without using bytecode
To replace the JVM so programs run faster
To provide a unified environment with code editor, compiler, debugger, and build tools in one application
To provide a runtime environment for executing Java programs on servers
questions.explanation: An IDE integrates multiple development tools into a single application: a code editor with syntax highlighting, a compiler/build system, a debugger, code completion, project management, and more. It does not replace the JDK or JVM — it uses them under the hood. Popular Java IDEs include IntelliJ IDEA, Eclipse, and NetBeans.
hints: Think about what 'Integrated' means in the name
An IDE combines tools that you could also use separately from the command line

questions.title: Java Primitive Types Count
questions.description: How many primitive data types does Java have?
options: 8 (byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean)
questions.explanation: Java has exactly 8 primitive data types: byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), int (32-bit), long (64-bit), float (32-bit), double (64-bit), char (16-bit Unicode), and boolean. String and arrays are not primitive types — they are reference types. Every other type in Java is a reference type (object).
hints: There are four integer types, two floating-point types, and two other types
String is NOT a primitive type in Java

questions.title: Variable Declaration and Initialization
questions.description: What happens if you try to use a local variable in Java without initializing it?
options: A compilation error occurs — Java requires local variables to be initialized before use
The variable is automatically initialized to 0 or null
A runtime warning is printed but the program continues
The variable contains a random garbage value
questions.explanation: Unlike instance variables (which get default values), local variables in Java must be explicitly initialized before they are used. If you try to use an uninitialized local variable, the compiler will generate an error such as 'variable might not have been initialized'. This is a safety feature that prevents bugs caused by undefined values.
hints: Java has stricter rules about initialization than C
There is a difference between local variables and instance variables regarding defaults

questions.title: String Concatenation with +
questions.description:
What is the output of the following Java code?

System.out.println("Value: " + 5 + 3);
questions.explanation: When the + operator is used with a String, it performs string concatenation from left to right. First, "Value: " + 5 produces the string "Value: 5". Then "Value: 5" + 3 produces "Value: 53". The integers are converted to strings and concatenated, not added. To get 8, you would need parentheses: "Value: " + (5 + 3).
hints: The + operator associates left to right
When one operand is a String, + performs concatenation

questions.title: Classpath Purpose
questions.description: What is the purpose of the classpath in Java?
options: It defines the order in which methods are called in a program
It specifies the path where Java source files should be saved
It tells the JVM where to find compiled .class files and libraries
It sets the maximum memory the JVM can use
questions.explanation: The classpath is an environment variable or command-line argument that tells the JVM and Java compiler where to look for user-defined classes and packages. When you run 'java -cp /path/to/classes MyApp', you specify that the JVM should search /path/to/classes for the compiled MyApp.class file and any other classes it depends on.
hints: Think about what the JVM needs to find in order to execute a program
It is analogous to the PATH environment variable for executables

questions.title: Type Casting — Narrowing
questions.description: What happens when you write: int x = (int) 3.9;
options: A compilation error occurs because double cannot be converted to int
x is assigned the value 4 (rounded up)
x is assigned the value 3 (the decimal part is truncated)
questions.explanation: Casting a double to int in Java performs truncation, not rounding. The decimal part is simply removed, so 3.9 becomes 3. This is called a narrowing conversion and requires an explicit cast — without (int), the compiler would report an error because data could be lost. If you want rounding, use Math.round() instead.
hints: Explicit casting truncates rather than rounds
Java does not allow implicit narrowing conversions

questions.title: Escape Sequences in Java Strings
questions.description: What does the escape sequence \n represent in a Java string?
options: The literal characters backslash and n
questions.explanation: In Java strings, \n is the escape sequence for a newline character, which moves the output cursor to the next line. Other common escape sequences include \t (tab), \\ (literal backslash), \" (literal double quote), and \' (literal single quote). These are used to represent special characters that cannot be typed directly in string literals.
hints: The backslash indicates an escape sequence, not a literal character
This is the same convention used in C, C++, and many other languages

questions.title: Java Package Declaration
questions.description: What is the purpose of a package declaration like 'package com.example.myapp;' at the top of a Java file?
options: It organizes the class into a namespace to avoid naming conflicts and structure the project
It imports all classes from the com.example.myapp library
It specifies the version of Java required to compile the file
It declares that the file depends on the com.example.myapp framework
questions.explanation: A package declaration groups related classes into a namespace, which helps avoid naming conflicts between classes with the same name from different libraries. The package also corresponds to the directory structure: com.example.myapp means the .java file must be in the directory com/example/myapp/. Packages also control access with package-private visibility.
hints: Packages relate to the directory structure on disk
They prevent naming collisions when different libraries have classes with the same name

questions.title: JIT Compilation
questions.description: What is JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation in the context of the JVM?
options: The JVM compiles frequently executed bytecode into native machine code at runtime for better performance
The JVM compiles Java source code (.java) into bytecode (.class) on the fly
The JVM precompiles all bytecode before the program starts
The JVM compiles native machine code back into bytecode for portability
questions.explanation: JIT compilation is a JVM optimization technique where frequently executed ("hot") bytecode is compiled into native machine code during program execution. This gives near-native performance while maintaining the portability of bytecode. The JVM initially interprets bytecode, then identifies hot spots and compiles them to native code for faster subsequent execution.
hints: It is an optimization that happens during runtime, not before
It bridges the performance gap between interpreted and compiled languages

questions.title: Multiple Classes in One File
questions.description: Can a single .java file contain multiple class definitions?
options: No, each .java file can only contain exactly one class
Yes, but only one class can be declared as public, and the file must be named after that public class
Yes, and all classes can be public
Yes, but the file must be named after the first class listed
questions.explanation: A single .java file can contain multiple class definitions, but at most one of them can be declared as public. The file name must match the name of the public class. The non-public classes in the same file will each produce their own .class file when compiled. In practice, it is considered best practice to put each class in its own file.
hints: Think about the naming rule: file name must match the public class
Non-public classes do not have the file naming restriction

questions.title: System.out.printf Formatting
questions.description: What is the output of System.out.printf("%.2f", 3.14159);?
questions.explanation: The format specifier %.2f means: print a floating-point number with exactly 2 decimal places. 3.14159 rounded to 2 decimal places is 3.14 (the third decimal digit 1 is less than 5, so it rounds down). System.out.printf works similarly to printf in C and uses the same format specifiers.
hints: The .2 in %.2f specifies the number of decimal places
printf performs rounding when limiting decimal places

questions.title: Compilation Error — Type Mismatch
questions.description: Which line causes a compilation error?
options: Line 4 — println cannot print an int
Line 2 — 3.14 is not a valid double literal
Line 1 — int cannot be assigned a value
Line 3 — cannot assign a double result to an int without explicit casting
questions.explanation: On Line 3, x + y evaluates to a double (because y is a double, and int + double promotes to double). Assigning a double to an int is a narrowing conversion that could lose data, so Java requires an explicit cast: int z = (int)(x + y);. This is a compile-time error, not a runtime error — the compiler catches it before the program can run.
hints: When you add an int and a double, the result is a double
Java does not allow implicit narrowing conversions

questions.title: What is an Object in OOP?
questions.description: In object-oriented programming, what is an object?
options: A function that can be called from anywhere in the program
An instance of a class that has state (fields) and behavior (methods)
A data type like int or double
questions.explanation: In OOP, an object is a concrete instance of a class. It has state, represented by its fields (also called attributes or instance variables), and behavior, represented by its methods. For example, if 'Car' is a class, then a specific car like 'myToyota' is an object. A class is the blueprint; an object is the thing built from that blueprint.
hints: Think about the relationship between a blueprint and a building
An object combines data and the operations that act on that data

questions.title: What is a Class in OOP?
questions.description: What is a class in Java?
options: A built-in data type like int or boolean
A running instance of a program
A blueprint or template that defines the fields and methods that objects of that type will have
questions.explanation: A class in Java is a template or blueprint that defines what data (fields) and behaviors (methods) its objects will have. You can create multiple objects (instances) from a single class, each with its own values for the fields. For example, a Student class might define fields like name and grade, and methods like getGPA().
hints: A class describes what an object looks like and what it can do
Multiple objects can be created from the same class

questions.title: InputMismatchException with Scanner
questions.description: What happens if the user types "hello" when the program calls sc.nextInt()?
options: The value 0 is assigned to the variable
An InputMismatchException is thrown at runtime
The Scanner waits until the user enters a valid integer
questions.explanation: If the input cannot be parsed as an integer, Scanner's nextInt() method throws an InputMismatchException at runtime. This is a runtime error, not a compilation error, because the compiler cannot know what the user will type. Proper programs should handle this with try-catch blocks or use hasNextInt() to check before reading.
hints: The compiler cannot predict what the user will enter
Scanner methods throw exceptions when input does not match the expected type

questions.title: Java Compilation and Execution Stages
questions.description: What is the correct sequence of stages when you write, compile, and run a Java program?
options: Write .java source -> JVM converts to .class -> javac compiles .class to native code -> program executes
Write .java source -> javac compiles to .class bytecode -> JVM interprets/JIT-compiles bytecode to native code -> program executes
Write .java source -> javac compiles directly to native machine code -> OS executes the binary
Write .java source -> JVM compiles to .class -> IDE converts to native code -> program executes
questions.explanation: The correct sequence is: (1) The developer writes Java source code in a .java file. (2) The javac compiler compiles the source into platform-independent bytecode stored in a .class file. (3) The JVM loads the bytecode and either interprets it or uses JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation to convert frequently executed sections to native machine code. (4) The program executes on the target platform. This two-stage compilation is key to Java's portability.
hints: There is an intermediate step between source code and machine execution
The JVM can both interpret and compile bytecode

questions.title: args Parameter in main
questions.description: What does the 'args' parameter in 'public static void main(String[] args)' contain when you run: java MyProgram hello world?
options: args[0] is "java", args[1] is "MyProgram", args[2] is "hello", args[3] is "world"
args[0] is "MyProgram", args[1] is "hello", and args[2] is "world"
args[0] is "hello" and args[1] is "world" — it contains command-line arguments
args is empty because no arguments were explicitly passed with a flag
questions.explanation: The String[] args parameter receives command-line arguments passed after the class name. When you run 'java MyProgram hello world', args[0] is "hello" and args[1] is "world". Unlike C's argv, Java's args does NOT include the program name — it only contains the arguments. args.length would be 2 in this case.
hints: Unlike C, Java does not include the program name in the arguments array
The arguments start from index 0

questions.title: Tracing Output — Multiple print Statements
questions.description: What is the output of the following program?
questions.explanation: Let us trace through: print("A") outputs 'A' with no newline. println("B") outputs 'B' followed by a newline. print("C") outputs 'C' on the new line with no newline. print("D") outputs 'D' immediately after 'C'. The result is 'AB' on the first line and 'CD' on the second line.
hints: print() does not add a newline; println() does
Trace each statement and track the cursor position

questions.title: Java char Type and Unicode
questions.description: What is the output of the following Java code?
questions.explanation: In Java, char is a 16-bit numeric type representing a Unicode character. When you use + with a char and an int, the char is promoted to int via its Unicode value. 'A' has the Unicode (and ASCII) value 65, so c + 1 evaluates to the integer 66. The result is an int, so println prints the number 66, not the character 'B'. To get 'B', you would need to cast: (char)(c + 1).
hints: char is a numeric type in Java — arithmetic is performed on its integer value
The + operator with char and int produces an int result

questions.title: Garbage Collection in Java
questions.description: How does Java handle memory deallocation for objects?
options: Memory is released when the variable goes out of scope, similar to stack allocation in C
The programmer must manually call free() or delete to release memory
The JVM's garbage collector automatically reclaims memory when objects are no longer referenced
Objects are never deallocated — memory is only freed when the program terminates
questions.explanation: Java uses automatic garbage collection: the JVM periodically identifies objects that are no longer reachable (i.e., no active references point to them) and reclaims their memory. Unlike C/C++, Java does not require manual memory deallocation — there is no free() or delete operator. This eliminates common bugs like memory leaks and dangling pointers, though the programmer can still cause logical memory leaks by holding unnecessary references.
hints: Java was designed to avoid the manual memory management problems in C and C++
The JVM handles memory cleanup automatically

questions.title: String Comparison — == vs equals()
questions.description: What is the output of the following code?
questions.explanation: The == operator compares object references (memory addresses), not content. Since 'a' and 'b' are created with 'new', they are distinct objects in memory, so a == b is false. The equals() method compares the actual character content of the strings, so a.equals(b) is true because both contain "hello". This distinction between identity (==) and equality (equals) is fundamental in Java.
hints: == checks if two references point to the same object in memory
equals() checks if the content of two objects is logically the same

questions.title: Encapsulation Overview
questions.description: Which OOP concept is described as 'bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit, and restricting direct access to the data'?
questions.explanation: Encapsulation is the OOP principle of bundling data (fields) and behavior (methods) together in a class, while hiding the internal state from outside code. In Java, this is achieved by declaring fields as private and providing public getter and setter methods. Encapsulation protects the integrity of an object's state and reduces coupling between components.
hints: This concept is about data hiding and protecting internal state
It involves using access modifiers like private and public

questions.title: The Four Pillars of OOP
questions.description: Which of the following lists all four fundamental principles (pillars) of Object-Oriented Programming?
questions.explanation: The four pillars of OOP are: (1) Encapsulation — hiding internal state and requiring interaction through methods. (2) Inheritance — creating new classes based on existing ones. (3) Polymorphism — allowing objects to be treated as instances of their parent type. (4) Abstraction — hiding complex implementation details and showing only essential features. Recursion and serialization are programming concepts but not OOP pillars.
hints: These are the foundational principles that define the OOP paradigm
Compilation and recursion are general programming concepts, not specific to OOP

questions.title: Scanner — Detecting Available Input
questions.description: Which Scanner method checks if there is another integer available to read without actually consuming the input?
questions.explanation: The hasNextInt() method returns true if the next token in the Scanner's input can be interpreted as an int, without actually reading or consuming the token. This is useful for validating input before reading it, preventing InputMismatchException. Scanner provides similar methods for all types: hasNextDouble(), hasNextLine(), hasNext(), etc.
hints: Scanner 'has' methods check for the presence of data without consuming it
The naming pattern matches the 'next' methods: hasNextInt() pairs with nextInt()

questions.title: Java Compilation Error — Mismatched Braces
questions.description: What error occurs when you try to compile this code?
options: A compilation error: the class body is missing a closing brace
A runtime error: the program crashes when the JVM tries to execute it
No error: the program compiles and runs normally
A warning but the program still compiles
questions.explanation: The code is missing the closing brace '}' for the class body. The method's closing brace closes the method, but the class itself is left open. The Java compiler will report an error such as 'reached end of file while parsing'. Mismatched braces are one of the most common syntax errors, especially in larger programs. Using proper indentation and an IDE that highlights matching braces helps avoid this.
hints: Count the opening and closing braces to see if they match
The compiler checks that all code blocks are properly closed
